reason_thou:
1. Understanding the Issue:
The crash manifests as an assertion failure in BoUpSLP::removeInstructionsAndOperands, specifically:
  (I->use_empty() || all_of(I->uses(), [&](Use &U) { return isDeleted(cast<Instruction>(U.getUser())); }))
This function drops references of instructions we intend to delete, and then asserts that either the instruction has no users or all users are themselves marked for deletion. In this reproducer, that invariant is violated: some users of an instruction being erased are not marked in DeletedInstructions at the time of the check, causing the abort.

2. Analyzing opt's Log:
The SLP logs show a sequence of horizontal reduction vectorizations, erasing various scalar ops (adds, phis, extracts) in two waves. During the first reduction, many scalars are "Erasing scalar:" and some users are noted as "Internal user will be removed" but not immediately marked deleted. The second reduction continues and then attempts to erase PHIs and extractelements, ultimately hitting the assert in removeInstructionsAndOperands after dropAllReferences.
The core pattern is:
- We add initial DeadVals to DeletedInstructions.
- We drop all references of I.
- Then we assert all users of I are marked deleted.
However, the pass updates/erases a web of dependent instructions incrementally. Some users of I are still live (not yet in DeletedInstructions) when this function reaches the assert, even though they will be removed later in the same overall transformation sequence.

3. Root Cause Analysis:
The function assumes a strong invariant: before physical removal of an instruction, either all its uses have been rewritten away, or all users are marked for deletion. In horizontal reduction vectorization in this reproducer, we are erasing a set of instructions where the users are part of the same transformation but not yet marked DeletedInstructions. removeInstructionsAndOperands only pre-marks the DeadVals themselves into DeletedInstructions (at the start), but it does not pre-mark their users. As a result, when we check the invariant, some users of I are not yet in DeletedInstructions, violating the assert, even though they are about to be removed in subsequent steps.
Thus, the problem is a missed pre-marking of users that are being implicitly deleted as part of the current removal operation. Since dropAllReferences does not clear the use list, the assert must rely on DeletedInstructions containing those users. We need to ensure that all users of the instructions we are erasing are marked as deleted before the assert runs, to maintain the invariant.

4. Proposed Edit Point(s):
We should update removeInstructionsAndOperands to proactively mark all instruction users of the DeadVals as deleted before the assert block. This matches the intended contract: any instruction slated for deletion should have its users either rewritten or themselves marked for deletion. The change is localized and preserves the existing logic.

Edit at llvm/lib/Transforms/Vectorize/SLPVectorizer.cpp lines 2648-2668:
- After salvaging debug info and before I->dropAllReferences(), iterate over I->uses() and, for each user that is an Instruction, insert it into DeletedInstructions. This ensures the subsequent assert sees all remaining users as "deleted" as per the set, aligning with the invariant.

Concretely, add:
  for (Use &UseRef : I->uses()) {
    if (auto *UserI = dyn_cast<Instruction>(UseRef.getUser()))
      DeletedInstructions.insert(UserI);
  }
just before I->dropAllReferences().

This change does not erase the users immediately; it only marks them, which is consistent with the delayed deletion model of BoUpSLP and the assert expectations. Users that should remain live must have had their operands replaced before this point, in which case they would not be in the use-list of I and this marking does nothing. Users that are part of the same removal wave are now properly marked deleted.

5. Conclusion:
The crash is due to removeInstructionsAndOperands asserting that all users of an instruction being removed are marked as deleted, but the code did not pre-mark those users. By marking all instruction users of DeadVals into DeletedInstructions before dropping references and removal, we satisfy the invariant and prevent the assertion failure. This aligns with the delayed deletion design and with the pass semantics in the face of complex reduction vectorization transformations.
